title: Fooling AI: Adversarial Attacks
concept: Adversarial attacks introduce imperceptible perturbations to images, causing neural networks to make high-confidence mistakes.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the primary focus of industrial vision engineering before writing code?

options.text: Controlling lighting, camera angles, and physical triggers
Using the largest possible learning rate

exercises.explain: Setting up physical lighting and camera alignment resolves most industrial CV challenges before model training.

cards.title: An optical illusion for computers
cards.body: Imagine looking at a photo of a panda, but a computer looks at it and is 99% sure it's a gibbon monkey. To your eyes, the image didn't change at all! This is an adversarial attack — an intentional glitch designed to trick AI.

cards.title: What is an Adversarial Attack?
cards.body: An adversarial attack is a technique that applies tiny, calculated changes (perturbations) to an input image. These changes are invisible to humans but disrupt the model's inner activations, causing misclassification.

cards.title: Whispering in code
cards.body: Think of an adversarial attack like a secret whistle that only dogs can hear. Humans just hear normal background wind, but the dog (the neural network) hears a loud command that makes it change its behavior completely.

cards.title: The panda and the noise
cards.body: Researchers take a clean panda image, calculate a noise pattern using model gradients, and add it. The resulting image looks identical, but a state-of-the-art classifier labels it a "gibbon" with 99.3% confidence.

cards.title: Not just random noise
cards.body: You might think adding any random static or camera noise will fool an AI. In reality, random noise rarely works; adversarial perturbations must be mathematically calculated specifically to target the model's weak points.

left.text:
Clean pixel values
Activation maps show panda features
Model output: Panda (99%)
Safe for deployment

right.text:
Clean + tiny gradient noise
Activations disrupted by noise
Model output: Gibbon (99%)
Invisible to human eyes

exercises.prompt: What is an adversarial perturbation in computer vision?

options.text: A tiny, calculated pixel modification designed to fool a model
An augmentation method that rotates images by 90 degrees
A compression step that converts floats to integers

exercises.explain: Perturbations are precise mathematical adjustments made to pixels to trigger model errors.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Adversarial attacks are easily noticed by humans because they turn images into messy, garbled noise.
exercises.explain: False. Good adversarial perturbations are mathematically calculated to be imperceptible (invisible) to human eyes.

exercises.sentence: Calculated noise designed to trick a neural network is an ___ attack.
exercises.explain: Adversarial attacks exploit mathematical weaknesses in neural network decision boundaries.

exercises.prompt: Why do standard neural networks fall victim to adversarial attacks?

options.text: They rely on high-frequency pixel patterns that are easily manipulated by precise noise
They do not have enough convolutional layers
They only process grayscale images in production

exercises.explain: Networks learn patterns that are different from human perception, including subtle pixel relationships that attackers can exploit.

exercises.prompt: You are deploying a security camera system that classifies delivery trucks. You want to test if a competitor could easily trick your model by editing the digital pixels of their truck photos. What is this threat category called?
exercises.explain: Manipulating pixels mathematically to force a model error is a classic digital adversarial attack.
